---
title: "Making your first GitHub repo (and GitHub page)"
author: "Jess Kunke"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: cosmo
embed-resources: true
---

## Overview and prerequisites

In this tutorial, I'll show you how to...

- make a new GitHub repository (repo),
- create a new R Studio project on your computer that is connected to the GitHub repo,
- 

## Making a new GitHub repo

Go to github.com and log in. Click the + sign near the upper right of the window, and select "New Repository":

![](images/03_annotated.png)

Enter a name and (optionally) a description for your repository:

![](images/05.png)

Scroll down, leaving the rest of the settings as they are for now, and click the green "Create repository" button:

![](images/06.png)

Congratulations, you are now the proud owner of a new repository! Click the "copy" symbol button indicated below to copy the URL for cloning (making a copy of) your repository to your computer:

![](images/07_annotated.png)

For now, leave this page up in your browser (we'll return to it later), and we'll move to R Studio for the next few steps.



## Creating and linking an R Studio Project to your repo

Whether you open R Studio fresh now or you already have R Studio open to another project, either way is fine. In my screenshots below, you can see I already had an R Studio project open called STAT408_Spr26, and creating the new project did not disturb this project.

In R Studio, navigate to the top menu and click File > New Project... as shown below, then select Version Control, then Git: 

![](images/10.png)

![](images/11.png)

![](images/12.png)

You should get a window titled "Clone Git Repository", as shown below. Paste the URL you copied from GitHub in the "Repository URL" box. Double-click the part of the URL that is your repo name (here, that's `demo_repo`) and do Control-C (Windows) or Command-C (Mac) to copy it; this will automatically paste it under "Project directory name", or else you can paste it yourself there. Click "Browse" to select the folder on your computer that you want to contain this repository; in my example below, the `demo_repo` folder will be a subfolder of my `Documents` folder. Finally, check the "Open in new session" box and click "Create Project".

![](images/14.png)

R Studio should open a fresh R Studio session, a new window with a new environment and a new instance of R running, and the title at the top of the window should be the name of your repo. Here, you can see my window is titled "demo_repo".

![](images/15.png)
If you click on the "Git" tab toward the upper right, you'll see that we have some untracked files:

![](images/16.png)

When we created the repo on GitHub, we just made an empty repo. When we made it into an R Studio project, R Studio added an R project file (demo_repo.Rproj) and a .gitignore file. What are these?

- The R project file allows you to open your R project in the future and have the working directory automatically set to that folder. To do this, rather than opening R Studio from the application, you can just use Finder (Mac) or File Explorer (Windows) or whatever file navigator you have to navigate to your project folder on your computer, then double-click the demo_repo.Rproj file or whatever your R project file is called (its name will be the same as your R project and it will have the extension .Rproj). This will open your project in R Studio.
- The .gitignore file allows you not to track certain files in your folder. Anything you list in this file, git will ignore, and it won't get stored on GitHub. Each file (or file pattern) you want not to track should be on a separate line.

![](images/17.png)

I'm going to add two more files to the .gitignore file. I only track the R project file in my repository if I think other users (including future me) will want to use it as an R project, so for this project I won't track it. Since I'm on a Mac, I also avoid tracking the .DS_Store file that Macs generate to track folder attributes. What files you add here will depend on your project, your computer, your intended audience/purposes, and your preferences. Notice that since I stopped tracking the R project file by adding it to .gitignore, the Git panel in the upper right of the R Studio window no longer lists it; it's ignoring that file.

![](images/18.png)


## The basic git cycle: status, add, commit, push

Let's make some files now in this repo. Suppose I want to create an R script with some code, so I click the document-with-green-plus-sign symbol and select "R script" to open a blank R script (.R extension), then add some code. You can add whatever code you like here, or you can copy my code from the code chunk below the screenshot.

![](images/19.png)

```{r}
#| eval: false

library(tidyverse)
library(nycflights13)

flights |> 
  filter(dest == "IAH") |> 
  mutate(speed = distance / air_time * 60) |> 
  select(year:day, dep_time, carrier, flight, speed) |> 
  arrange(desc(speed))

fast_flights = flights |> 
  filter(dest == "IAH") |> 
  mutate(speed = distance / air_time * 60) |> 
  select(year:day, dep_time, carrier, flight, speed) |> 
  arrange(desc(speed))
```

I'll save this script as sample_script.R:

![](images/20.png)

Now, the Git panel on the right says we have two untracked files: .gitignore and sample_script.R. That's because both these files have been created and saved in our demo_repo folder, and git detects their existence and is letting us know that neither is being tracked by git yet. (This information is the same information we get if we type `git status` in the Terminal. We'll talk more about this and other commands at the end of this section.)

![](images/21.png)

To track the files and commit the changes we made, we'll first click the double-page-with-checkmark symbol (circled in the first screenshot below, and it will say "Commit" next to it if your panel is wide enough to show the name of the button). This will open up a new window (second screenshot below). Select the checkboxes for both files, enter a short message about what changes we've made that we're tracking, and click the "Commit" button on this smaller window:

![](images/21_annotated.png)

![](images/22.png)

That should give you a window like this, and you can hit "Close". If instead it tells you there was an error, then the commit didn't work and you need to do some troubleshooting.

![](images/23.png)

That brings you back to this window, and before closing out of this window, we have one more step to do. So far, we tracked the changes we made on our local computer, but we haven't synced those changes with the remote repo on GitHub. To do that, we need to push the changes to GitHub by clicking "Push":

![](images/24.png)

Which should bring up a window like this, which you can close after you check that it doesn't mention any errors.

![](images/25.png)

To check that the changes pushed successfully to GitHub, toggle back to your browser to the page where your repo is and refresh the page. (If you closed that page in your brower, you can just reenter the URL to your repo, which will be `github.com/<your username>/<your repo name>`.) You should see some files there now, and they should match whatever you just committed and pushed.

![](images/26.png)

Awesome! What we did using the R Studio graphical user interface (GUI, point-and-click) can equivalently be done entirely in the Terminal pane using the command line interface (CLI, typing text commands) via these four commands:

```{git}
#| eval: false

git status
git add --all
git commit -m "Created a sample script"
git push
```

Sometimes the commands work faster and with fewer errors in the command line, so they are handy to know.

- For example, if I have made changes to a lot of files and I want to commit those changes, using the GUI (clicking boxes in the Git panel) requires me to click the box next to each file, whereas with the Terminal I can just type `git add --all`.
- Additionally, the file size limit for a commit is higher for the GUI (point-and-click in the Git panel) than the CLI (typing commands in the Terminal), and it takes longer, so if I'm adding data or bigger files, I use the Terminal.

Anatomy of a git command: Each git command has the format `git command`, where `command` is replaced by the name of the command (like `status` or `add`). Some commands like `add` and `commit` have optional or required arguments/inputs you can pass to them, which are called *flags* or *options*. To add these, you provide the name of the flag, and if it requires user input, you put that input after the flag. If you use the abbreviated name, it's a single dash, as in `git commit -m "Created a sample script"`, where `-m` stands for message and the part in quotes afterward is the commit message you would like git to use. If you use the full flag name, you use a double dash, as in `git add --all`, where the flag `--all` tells the `add` command to track all the changes that have been made.


## Making a GitHub page for your project

For each public GitHub repo you make on your account, you can make an associated webpage for it. Let's try that with our current repo. Suppose that we want to make an online tutorial or demo for the code we wrote, sample_script.R.

To start, we create a new Quarto document by clicking that same new document symbol in the upper left, selecting "Quarto Document...", and clicking "Create Empty Document":

![](images/27.png)

![](images/28.png)

That should give you something like this:

![](images/29.png)

Let's copy and paste the code from our script, change the title, and add a little text:

![](images/30.png)

When you save the file, you should notice that the Git panel reflects that there is a new untracked file:

![](images/31.png)

I'll show a slightly different way to track and commit this file from what I showed before. Rather than going straight to the Git panel Commit button (the double-document-with-checkmark symbol), I'll click the checkmark next to the new file first, then click the Git panel Commit button, add a commit message, and click "Commit":

![](images/32.png)

![](images/33.png)

Once again, you can close the window, click "Push", then close the window again (just check for errors in both steps). Great, now let's go back to the browser and refresh the page for the repo, and you should see your code_demo.qmd file there:

![](images/36.png)

Now it's time to tell GitHub that we want to activate a webpage for this repo. Click the Settings button with the gear symbol on the right end of the top GitHub menu, then click Pages on the lefthand menu:

![](images/37.png)

Under Branch, click the dropdown menu that says None and choose main, then hit save. You should see this:

![](images/39.png)

GitHub is now processing your new page! Click Actions along the top GitHub menu and you'll see a brown dot next to the action it is running to get your page ready:

![](images/40.png)

This brown dot means the action is pending, meaning it's working on it.  When your page is ready, it will turn to a green checkmark like this:

![](images/41.png)

If you click "pages build and deployment", you'll see more details about the action, as well as a link to your page:

![](images/42.png)

Unfortunately when we click the link, we get an error message instead of a website:

![](images/43.png)

This is because the page that GitHub Pages will use for your project page has to be an HTML file called index.html, not a Quarto document called code_demo.qmd. So we need to (1) name our Quarto file index.qmd and (2) render it to get index.html before pushing. Let's do that in R Studio:

![](images/44.png)

![](images/45.png)

When you do that, your Git panel should now show that you've "deleted" your old code_demo.qmd file and "created" an untracked file called "index.qmd"; this is what renaming a file looks like.

![](images/46.png)

Go ahead and commit these changes as you normally would. You'll notice that if you're using the GUI approach (clicking the checkboxes to add the files), it recognizes that all you did was rename a file, and the Git panel list of changes updates accordingly:

![](images/47.png)

I'll call my commit "Rename demo to index". After I commit and push, I toggle back to my browser and see that the renaming is reflected on GitHub too, and there's a brown "pending" dot next to the lastest commit, indicating it's updating my page:

![](images/51.png)

When my page is updated, that brown dot will turn into a green checkmark:

![](images/52.png)

But the page still won't work because we didn't render the Quarto file to an HTML file! So let's do that finally by clicking the Render button above my Quarto document in the R Studio Source panel:

![](images/53.png)

Notice that now there are multiple new files, not just the HTML file but also a whole folder called index_files. When you are rendering to HTML to put it on GitHub, I generally recommend using the option `embed-resources: true` so that all the information in index_files gets included in the HTML file itself. I demonstrate that here, and after rendering the Quarto file again, I see that the HTML file is the only new file (shown in the status column as two yellow question marks) and the Quarto file has been modified (shown in the status column as a blue "M").

![](images/56.png)

If you check the checkboxes to stage the changes for commit, you'll see that

- the double question marks become a green "A" for "added" and is on the lefthand side of the Status column, and
- the blue "M" stays a blue "M" but moves to the left.

![](images/57.png)

This reflects that both files have been staged for commit. Go ahead and finish your commit and push, then check your repo, and it should look like this:

![](images/58.png)

Once the brown dot turns to a green checkmark as before, go to your project page URL github, which will be `<your username>.github.io/<your repo name>`, and you'll see something like this:

![](images/60.png)

Hurray!

One last step: we can make the project page URL easier to find from our repo page. Go back to your GitHub repo at `github.com/<your username>/<your repo name>`, and click the gear symbol next to "About":

![](images/61_annotated.png)

Check the box that says "Use your GitHub Pages website":

![](images/62.png)

Now your URL appears on your repo page in the About section!

![](images/64.png)
